speaker: interviewer
text: Alfred Gottschalk. What was life like for your parents in those years? It sounds like you were integrating into American society. Was that true of them also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents really had a great difficulty integrating into American society. Because most of their life was caught up in work. My mother eventually worked in a defense factory and made plastic raincoats for airmen jackets. And I remember sometimes she would come home, and her hands would be bloody red, because of the fabric that she was working on, as an-- a machine operator, was so course that her fingers never healed. And she was doing piecework, which means that you didn't get a salary, but you get a salary for every piece of work that you did. It was like a commission thing. And the more you worked, the harder you-- the more money you got. And I remember one week she was so pleased she finally made $37, which was for her, a lot of money. And my dad, until he became very ill, worked, I think, for a while as a butcher. And then he also worked in an airplane manufacturing place. And he was doing defense work. And it was very hard for him to work on Shabbos. But to defeat Hitler, he did. But to keep the salary for Shabbos, he wouldn't. So he would prorate his salary that he earned on Shabbos, and give that to charity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's amazing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's quite a sacrifice.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was quite a man. So, he-- they were involved with-- with their own families and their own friends. Many of them didn't live in Brooklyn. Most of them lived in Washington Heights.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were their friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: People whom they knew from the vast Jewish network of the Rhineland, those who got out in the '30s and '40s. And Washington Heights was a place that seemed to be the community where most of them chose to settle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of the people with whom they had contact outside of the work setting, or even in the work setting--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were other refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were refugees, so life was centered on memories of-- for them of the country they left, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they centered so much on the memories of the country they left. They worried, and thought deal about the families they lost. And it was very difficult for them to really integrate. The major barrier was language. And the other was that the larger society was closed to them. Also there were, within the Jewish community, there were stratifications. The working people never mixed with the middle class. The middle class didn't mix with the blue-noses.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it wasn't just language that was a barrier. It was class.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was class, and education, society. My mother also worked for a while as a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she used to do house work between jobs. And they worked to survive, and expected their children to take care of themselves. My mother taught me how to cook, how to sew, how to wash my clothes. Because I had to do it. And so I learned how to do it from her, and I was able to take care of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been very hard. Your-- your parents were cultivated people. To be-- to not have access to-- to people of a similar cultural interest and so forth, was not that difficult, or-- did the class stratification, it must have been very painful?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's interesting. I remember as being without money, but not being poor. I mean, there was a tremendous amount of inner riches that was still there. And it was not just their generation, but it was a way of life that they were able to-- to live and forge, actually, in America. People who went-- relatives who went to other places, Baltimore, for example-- we had relatives-- had a much easier time with economic opportunities than those who went to New York. There was a Jewish Farm Bureau. And they were interested in having Jews leave New York, and go to places like South Carolina. And there was a farm waiting for us, they said, in South Carolina that we would just love. My father was ready to go, dying to get out of the factories. My mother said, under no circumstances am I going to bury myself in some [NON-ENGLISH] town as she called it, in South Carolina that she had never been to. [LAUGHTER] And then, you know, another thing my-- my dad tried also to get a job as a cantor, because he knew the liturgy. He had a nice voice, but not an operatic voice. And one day [SIGHS] he told me the story about-- this was in the eight months when he was trying to find his-- his way here. He went to a place called Cop Girardo to try out to be a cantor. And they were just miserable to him. And he kept talking about what nasty people these people in Cop Girardo were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day I'm flying on an airplane, and I pull out and an air map. I'm just looking. I say, oh my god, there's Cop Girardo, Cape Girardeau, Missouri. It became Cop Girardo.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, you know, it was they didn't know. That's why I have such great empathy. The other day I met with three Russian immigrants, who've been in Cincinnati for five years. One spoke and wrote poetry in English. Because he had learned English in school. One man who was a woodworker, could understand but couldn't speak. And the third spoke to me in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I said, you've got here replicated in a different generation what immigration was like in the '30s. It wasn't just German Jews, but Jews who escaped Eastern Europe. Non-Jews who came and got out had the same kind of struggle. And they grouped in the same neighborhoods. That's why they held together, because there was some security in the restricted neighborhoods.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for that generation, they were always basically in transition. Your generation, however, was able to move out into society and-- and become Americans, a little more easily it sounds like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my society was restricted too. I mean I came from a working-class family. The children at the temple, interestingly, they were all American. And my closest friend, who also became a rabbi, was the son of a candy store owner. And this Mr. Edwop was so wise. I wrote one of my compulsory high school compositions about him. I called him the candy store philosopher. So they had, the people whom we associated with were not the doctor's children, but the candy store's owner's son, and other laboring, children of laboring parents. There was-- later on in high school, it made very little difference. Because when you were dating, you met so many other types of kids. But serious relationships also had class lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: The word 'refugee' stays with you all your life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You-- you never stop thinking, if you've once been a refugee, you never stop thinking like a refugee. Because you're sure of nothing. There's no real permanence, and therefore, you're always thinking about-- as Jake Marcus used to say-- Dr. Marcus used to say, always figure out your retreat before you do the advance. And it's-- it's built into you-- into your psychology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you become a rather cautious person, thoughtful. You don't do rash things. You think through things very carefully before you move. Because you are a refugee. You know, they can ask you to leave one day. That stays in you. The war didn't break out until you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I once had-- by the way, I once had a talk with Kissinger about this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, yeah, and what-- oh yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- we were written up together in-- there was a German there is a German newspaper called And they did an issue called [SPEAKING GERMAN], the second generation. And we talked about this aspect of it at one time. And he said, yeah, me too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even-- having risen to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because him, I mean, Kissinger has this heavy accent.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he couldn't escape refugee status if he tried. You know, so he was there. He was facing it every day, as he listens to himself speak. And others listen to him. They know that he's not-- didn't come over on the number one. And number two, yeah, he wasn't born and raised in the-- the lap of American society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Learning English in Brooklyn, did you develop a Brooklyn accent, and?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean? [LAUGHTER] Everybody else has an accent. We-- we-- we in Brooklyn, never had accents.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHTER] The war broke out, or America entered the war then, the year you-- first year you were in college. And you went to a university where? In 1940, you go to university, and where did you go to university?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the war breaks out in '41, or America enters the war in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41, I'm still-- I'm still in public school. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: I beg your pardon.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm 11 years old--

speaker: interviewer
text: I was confused. I'm sorry. I was rushing ahead. OK. So you're 11 years old when the war breaks out. Did your life change with America's entrance into the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because then other people, neighbors became aware of the accent that you spoke with. And once America went into war against Germany, being a German refugee was no asset. In different parts of the America-- of the United States, German refugees, Jewish and others, were quarantined, in Los Angeles, for example. Under-- and there they had restrictions about going out in the evenings and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became aware of the fact that you were not only a refugee in America, but you were a German refugee. And your neighbors had to be, you know, persuaded that you weren't an enemy alien. Because those people were called enemy aliens. Japanese Americans were called, for a while, enemy aliens. So your origins became more important than-- than they ever were. Even though you had been a victim, and had-- and exiled by that very regime, you were nevertheless looked upon as potentially dangerous?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in England, for-- yes, in England, for example, German Jewish refugees were segregated. And some of them were sent to Australia, until the British could have sorted out who was a victim and who was a true enemy alien. But they were conscripted into the army, and fought in the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you personally respond to that-- that kind of treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't find it-- I-- I really don't remember that kind of discrimination against me, personally. By the time I got to Dewey Junior High School-- I was on the student newspaper, and in the clubs of the school. And I remember interviewing Mrs. Roosevelt.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, on what occasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my rabbi was President of the American Jewish Congress, and she was speaking to the Congress. And I pleaded with him, could I go along? So he said, sure. And he said, but you're on your own. And after she finished speaking, I went up to the dais, and I introduced myself. And I said, may I interview you for a few minutes, because I write for Dewy Junior High School's newspaper. And she gave me an interview on education, which had that wonderful line that the first thing we need to do is re-educate the teachers, which article made me very popular with my teachers at Dewey Junior High. [LAUGHTER] As a matter of fact, my English teacher called me in to ask me whether this was a correct quote. And I said, that's what she said. [LAUGHTER] And then the next sentence is, why did you have to write it, you know, kind of thing. I said, well, that's what she said. She-- that's what she thought the process had to begin. So I had-- I had a very interesting time at junior high school. There was a teacher of geography by the name of Friedman, who took an inordinate interest, and I became great at geography. And then Boys High School, my football coach was also chairman of the English and Spanish department, just a wonderful man with whom I corresponded until the year of his death. He was 90 years old, Wally Muller. And when my father died, I was in Boys High in 1946. And the team became my family. And it was a great experience. My high school experience pulled everything together for me. Because both the fellows I was with and the teachers were challenging. It was an academic-- one of two academic high schools in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Americanization somehow, because my language facility was good, my athletic was-- facilities-- abilities were more than acceptable, and I got along with people. And became a Hebrew teacher, which was the most difficult thing I ever did in my life. Why was it difficult?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teaching kids who don't-- who don't want--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I agree.

speaker: interviewee
text: --who don't want to learn. On a Sunday morning. [LAUGHTER] It's also very dangerous. You get hit with spit balls and all sorts of things. But that was an easier kind of integration to deal with than my parents had. They never really caught their breath in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were-- were teaching Hebrew school at that time. Did you have any inkling that you might wish to become a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was always there. And Rabbi Silverstein, who was the rabbi of my synagogue in New York, thought that I should go to yeshiva, which I did for a while, Chaim Berlin. But that was not for me. And then I began to seriously look into the Jewish Theological Seminary, their evening school, and I ended up at the Jewish Institute of Religion in New York, which I was really at home in, because of its freedom of restrictions, encouragement of independent thought, and very brilliant people with great ideas trying to instruct us in all kinds of interesting subjects-- Jewish history, literature, Hebrew language, Bible. And I loved it. It was that simple. It was like I was a-- a duck in the right pond.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you decided to become a rabbi. But first you went and took a-- a bachelor's degree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Hebrew Union doesn't admit you into rabbinic school until you have a bachelor's degree. I went to night school. HUC in New York had pre-rabbinic academy. So two nights a week I went to HUC at night. And I worked during the day, and went to college, and I lived on the subway, you know. [SIGHS] That's how you get everything done. I lived in Brooklyn. My school was two trains to Boys High School every day, and then to Manhattan for the college, then back to home, and work in between.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you loved what you were doing it sounds like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved what I was doing. I didn't know of any other way to do the things that I was doing. Because we were all absorbed in trying to stay alive physically, on the one hand, to fulfill our-- our objective goals. And the rabbinic Jewish scholarship always was something I was interested in. So was law and teaching. And as happens with impressionable people, the last greatest teacher you had is the field that you want to go into.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was--

speaker: interviewee
text: One man was a brilliant historian of the Chinese experience, Professor Levy. So that was at Brooklyn College, and that was excellent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you graduated Brooklyn College in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I graduated Brooklyn College in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And went on to HUC immediately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I took nine months, and went to Europe and Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during-- during the time you were in Europe, did you go back to Oberwesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us about that? This was the first time you'd been back since you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was involuntary. I again went with this childhood friend of mine, Stanley Edwop, and we went to Montreal to catch a ship, a student ship that took us to Europe. And we were going to go to Zeebrugge, Belgium, and from there to Paris, and Paris down to the Riviera, and from the Riviera to get on a boat at Genoa, to go to Palestine, then Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] got on this boat. And it hit a ship, somewhere near Zeebrugge, sprung a huge leak. And it was towed into the Bremerhaven harbor-- and-- where it was to be fixed. And there we got a compensation ticket to go to Paris, which would have been the ultimate stop. And the train went through Oberwesel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is where I had this great anxiety attack, as to whether or not to get off the train. And when the train landed, stopped at Oberwesel, Stanley and I grabbed our rucksacks, and we got off the train. And I remember being terribly excited, and not in a positive sense, just agitated, and wondered about what we should do. And my feet started to walk. And I went to-- from the train station to [INAUDIBLE], and stopped in front of the house. And I tried to get in. And the owner, the guy who bought it from my grandfather, now an old man, would not admit us. So I knocked at some other doors, and some people almost fell over when I told them who I was. Because I left a nine-year-old and came back a rather mature 22-year-old. So the next day we went up to the cemetery. We climbed up this rather formidable hill. And it was totally overgrown. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish cemetery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jewish cemetery. And we came down from the cemetery and stopped at-- somebody who had been a neighbor, who protested their love for the family and so forth. And I said, you know, I just came from the friedhof. I said, if you cared for the family, you wouldn't have permitted it to look like this. Of course, no one is responsible. They all had a difficult time during the war. They also had great losses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally Stanley said to me, yeah, let's get out of here. Let's go. And we left, rather quickly. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And went on to Paris, and then Genoa and Israel. Was that the only time you've been back to Oberwesel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went back with my wife about 10 years ago. We were on our way to a-- birthday celebration of a friend in Belgium, and decided to go back this way. And then at one time-- I-- it was really a third time. I went once-- my son, when he was coming back, he had spent a year in Israel. And he decided that he really had to go back with me to Oberwesel. And that was an involuntary return on my part. But since he wanted to, we then started to go through by going first to Nuremberg, and to recount the origins of Nazism. And then we drove to Oberwesel. And again, we went up to the cemetery. It was horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was still in disrepair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Disrepair, and visited some people. And again, this protestation of love and friendship and my son also said, like Stanley did, [LAUGHS] let's get out of here. And I didn't want to go back the third time at all. But I wanted to show it to Dee. There's some magnet, you know. And then I had two invitations to go back, both of which I turned down. One was to do a movie, on Werner and Alfred, two boys of Oberwesel. Of course one was from Bacharach, the one I referred to. The [NON-ENGLISH] Werner. And I thought about it. And I said, no, I don't want to do that. So I will probably never go back. There's no reason to. It's a city without Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The entire population was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gone. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, I know that there are people from Oberwesel who have gone back, those that have-- the handful of the Lichtensteins that survived. But that's as far as I know, there are no people there now.

speaker: interviewer
text: After, at least 400 years of your family-- and probably 1,000 years of Jewish settlement in that area, there are no Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Correct. There are no Jews. Well, that's true of most of Germany, except for Berlin and Frankfurt. The largest cities seem to have some kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] rebirth. There's some rebirth of Jewish life. But these are survivors, and the Jews from the Soviet Union. I mean who else could there be? And some Israelis who have left Israel, and started life in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there some continuity between that Alfred and this Alfred today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, sure. Scary continuity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you talk about it a bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure I fully understand it. I really probably pushed down a lot, as I've forgotten so much of the years that were formative years, let's say, from the age of five to the age of nine. It would take a lot of work to bring that out and back. And I'm not sure to what end at this point, except for self-understanding. I'm going to start writing. Because I find it-- I mean I talk to it easily. But writing requires a little more effort.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm going to start writing some things down as they occur to me, and then maybe weave them together at some point into something for my family, so we have a family history at least.

speaker: interviewer
text: Definitely. Now, on this first trip to Oberwesel, your ultimate goal was Palestine--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or it's Israel. And this was your first time there. Had you been part of the Zionist activities before you went to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I had belonged to a-- a revisionist group, called Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a relative a New York, Rabbi Louis I. Newman, who was a co-worker with the [NON-ENGLISH] of Menachem Begin. And he recruited us. I didn't know the difference between many of the Betar, between Betar and the Labor Zionists, and so forth, until we were in it. Then I was quite aware of what it was all about. And I kind of liked it. It was dramatic. It was interesting. It seemed to be doing something, and it had a philosophy, and an ideology. So I used to train with broomsticks.

speaker: interviewer
text: No really?

speaker: interviewee
text: Preparing for my Aliyah, my immigration to Israel. And I think the only time my father and I ever had a serious altercation was over my being part of Betar. He felt that he didn't want me to enter this right-wing organization. He was more inclined towards the blue-white group, which was Labor Zionist. And had he been successful in his immigration to Israel, while they were waiting in Amsterdam, that's probably where he would have gone, to either a kibbutz or some Labor Zionist oriented organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop here, and we'll pick up with your-- your time in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'm gonna take a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, a little break?

speaker: interviewee
text: --stretch on this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: How's it coming through?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's coming nicely.
